posterior_chain.title: Posterior Chain
posterior_chain.rationale_fallback: Runners are quad-dominant by default. Training the glutes, hamstrings, and lower back balances that out and protects your stride over long mileage.

single_leg_stability.title: Single-Leg Stability
single_leg_stability.rationale_fallback: Running is a single-leg sport. Stability work on one leg at a time translates directly to a more efficient stride and fewer compensations.

hip_glute.title: Hip & Glute Activation
hip_glute.rationale_fallback: Weak or lazy glute medius is the source of a lot of running pain (IT band, knee, hip). These are low-load activations you can do any day.

core_anti_rotation.title: Core & Anti-Rotation
core_anti_rotation.rationale_fallback: A stable trunk keeps your energy going forward instead of leaking sideways. Anti-rotation beats crunches for runners.

calf_achilles.title: Calf & Achilles Resilience
calf_achilles.rationale_fallback: Your calves and Achilles take the biggest single load in running. Progressive eccentric work builds tendons that can handle the mileage.

exercises.name: Glute Bridge
exercises.why_runners: Teaches your glutes to fire without your low back taking over — cheap insurance against IT band, knee, and hip pain.

exercises.name: Single-Leg Romanian Deadlift
exercises.search_query: single leg romanian deadlift runners form
exercises.why_runners: Trains the hamstrings and posterior chain under a single-leg balance load — exactly what running demands.

exercises.name: Barbell or Dumbbell Hip Thrust
exercises.why_runners: The strongest hip-extension exercise available. Builds the glutes to drive your stride — stop asking your hamstrings to do the glutes' job.

exercises.name: Good Morning
exercises.why_runners: Builds the hamstrings and low back that keep your posture intact late in a long run. Earn it with clean hinging form first.

exercises.name: Bulgarian Split Squat
exercises.why_runners: Single-leg strength with more range than most squats. Exposes imbalances between legs that running only hides.

exercises.name: Step-Up
exercises.search_query: step up exercise proper form runners
exercises.why_runners: Mimics the push-off phase of your stride. Drives quad and glute coordination with almost no skill barrier.

exercises.name: Single-Leg Squat to Box
exercises.search_query: single leg squat to box form
exercises.why_runners: A controlled single-leg squat teaches your knee to track over your toes — the exact pattern that breaks down with fatigue.

exercises.name: Pistol Squat Progression
exercises.why_runners: Full-range single-leg strength that punishes the whole chain — hips, knees, ankles, balance — all at once.

exercises.name: Clamshell
exercises.why_runners: Activates the glute medius — the small hip muscle that keeps your knee from collapsing inward on every stride.

exercises.name: Monster Walk (Banded)
exercises.why_runners: Keeps the glutes fighting against the band the whole time you move. Great primer for hip stability before a run.

exercises.name: Side-Lying Leg Raise
exercises.search_query: side lying leg raise glute medius
exercises.why_runners: Targets the glute medius directly. Boring but effective, especially for runners with hip or IT band issues.

exercises.name: Fire Hydrant
exercises.why_runners: Works hip abduction and rotation together. A staple for anyone with a cranky IT band or tight hip.

exercises.name: Single-Leg Glute Bridge
exercises.why_runners: The glute bridge minus the free leg — which means twice the load on the working side. A natural step before hip thrusts.

exercises.name: Pallof Press
exercises.why_runners: Teaches your core to resist rotation, which is what actually happens every time your foot hits the ground at speed.

exercises.name: Dead Bug
exercises.why_runners: A safe way to load the deep core while keeping the low back flat — useful if long mileage brings on low-back fatigue.

exercises.name: Bird Dog
exercises.why_runners: Trains you to move your limbs without losing trunk position — exactly what fails in the last miles of a long run.

exercises.name: Side Plank
exercises.why_runners: Hits the deep hip stabilizers and obliques together. One of the best single moves for runners with IT band flare-ups.

exercises.name: Suitcase Carry
exercises.why_runners: Walking with a weight in one hand forces your core to resist side-bending. Quiet but serious core work.

exercises.name: Eccentric Heel Drop
exercises.why_runners: The Alfredson protocol — clinically validated for Achilles tendinopathy. Slow, controlled drops rebuild tendon capacity.

exercises.name: Standing Calf Raise
exercises.why_runners: Builds the gastrocnemius — the calf muscle that handles most of your push-off on faster runs.

exercises.name: Seated Calf Raise (Soleus)
exercises.why_runners: Targets the soleus specifically — it takes the most load during steady-state running and is often the weak link.

exercises.name: Single-Leg Calf Raise
exercises.why_runners: Each calf takes your full bodyweight with every stride — train them that way rather than two at a time.

exercises.name: Toe Yoga / Short Foot
exercises.search_query: toe yoga short foot exercise runners
exercises.why_runners: Wakes up the small foot muscles that hold your arch. Unglamorous, but one of the few fixes that actually moves the needle on plantar and shin pain.

exercises.name: Kettlebell Swing
exercises.why_runners: Trains hip extension under explosive load — close to the power demand of a finishing kick or a hill sprint.

exercises.name: Reverse Lunge
exercises.why_runners: Less knee stress than a forward lunge, and it exposes single-leg weakness without heavy loading.

exercises.name: Copenhagen Plank
exercises.why_runners: Loads the adductors — the forgotten hip group — in a position runners almost never train otherwise.

exercises.name: Banded Standing Hip Abduction
exercises.why_runners: Glute medius work on your feet, against a band. Standing turns it into a whole-hip stability drill — not just an isolation move.

exercises.name: Tibialis Raise
exercises.why_runners: The front-of-shin muscle that shin splints love to wreck. Direct training here is one of the few things that actually helps.
